summary: Controls the official AppPrivacy capability-deny policy family only. The broader parent audit trail keeps the extra System policy write separate, while this child models the documented LetAppsAccess* ForceDeny values.

setting.name: App capability access policies
setting.casual_explanation: These policies stop Windows apps from accessing sensitive capabilities like camera, contacts, location, and background activity.
setting.why_people_change_it: People use this when they want a strict privacy posture for Windows apps.
setting.tradeoffs_overview: A broad deny policy can improve privacy, but it can also break normal app behavior and is more opinionated than targeted per-capability controls.
setting.professional_notes: AppPrivacy.admx documents many LetAppsAccess* policies with enum values including 0 for user control and 2 for force deny. This child record tracks the official AppPrivacy family only; the extra AllowUserInfoAccess System policy write remains in the deprecated parent audit trail.

allowed_values.label: Windows default
allowed_values.meaning: Leave capability access policies at the normal Windows baseline.

allowed_values.label: Current broad deny bundle
allowed_values.meaning: Apply the current broad set of ForceDeny values the app writes.

targets.notes: This child record models only the AppPrivacy policy family, not the extra System policy write from the broader parent bundle.

app_current_implementation.notes: The provider writes the official AppPrivacy ForceDeny bundle. The extra AllowUserInfoAccess System policy write is intentionally excluded from this child record and remains in the deprecated parent audit trail.

windows_defaults.label: Windows privacy-policy baseline
windows_defaults.applies_to: Windows 10 and later AppPrivacy policy surface

states.rationale: The broad bundle should not be treated as the baseline for general systems.

recommended_profiles.label: Windows default
intended_for: People who want capability prompts and app access decisions to remain more granular

states.rationale: Do not expose a very broad deny bundle as a default recommendation.

recommended_profiles.good_when: You want fewer surprise app breakages.
recommended_profiles.bad_when: You are deliberately pursuing a strict deny-all posture.

recommended_profiles.label: Current broad deny bundle

states.rationale: Track the broad deny behavior currently implemented by the app.

recommended_profiles.good_when: You are documenting the product's current privacy-hardening bundle.
recommended_profiles.bad_when: You want a smaller, clearly explained per-capability policy strategy.
tradeoffs: Higher chance of app regressions and user confusion.

evidence.title: Microsoft AppPrivacy administrative template
evidence.summary: The local AppPrivacy administrative template documents many LetAppsAccess* policy values, including 2 for ForceDeny and 0 for user control.

evidence.title: Current privacy provider broad deny bundle
evidence.summary: The provider writes a large AppPrivacy ForceDeny bundle. The extra System policy write remains in the deprecated parent audit trail.

validation_proof.notes: The local AppPrivacy.admx template documents the LetAppsAccess* policy family and its ForceDeny value semantics. The parent audit trail keeps the extra System policy write separate from this child record.

decision.why: The AppPrivacy policy family is official and machine-checkable, but the broad deny posture remains too opinionated for general users.
